query: Constructor vs ngOnInit in Angular: what is the difference?

competitors.label: Angular Component Lifecycle
competitors.selectionReason: The official lifecycle guide is the canonical source for constructor and ngOnInit timing, so it is the strongest docs baseline.
competitors.winReason: The lifecycle guide is the canonical baseline, but our page now answers the side-effect placement and the ngOnChanges-versus-ngOnInit follow-up more directly in one interview-shaped explanation.
theirStrengths: It states the official lifecycle order and clearly says ngOnInit runs after inputs are initialized.
theirGaps: It is less explicit about testing, side-effect placement, and why constructor work makes component setup harder to reason about.

realWorldUseCases: Putting side effects in the constructor makes component instantiation do "real work" before Angular finishes binding and before tests can set up spies.
followUpConfusion: If you must react to input changes over time, use ngOnChanges (or an input setter). DOM/ViewChild work usually belongs in ngAfterViewInit.

realWorldUseCases: The docs explain when constructor and ngOnInit run, but they are less opinionated about interview-grade placement of side effects and test setup.
actionableExamples: The lifecycle guide has enough timing detail that the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: Angular.dev still answers the input-initialization timing well, so the broader follow-up comparison remains close.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny example showing a constructor-based API call and the corrected ngOnInit version.
followUpConfusion: Add one explicit note on ngOnChanges coming before ngOnInit for bound inputs.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Basic Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: This is the main GreatFrontEnd Angular basics guide and a fair interview-prep comparator for lifecycle fundamentals.
competitors.winReason: Our page is more explicit about constructor side effects, input timing, testing implications, and where DOM or long-lived reactions belong next.
theirStrengths: It gives a concise lifecycle-hooks refresher for interview prep.
theirGaps: It does not go as far into constructor side effects, test timing, or which next hook to mention after ngOnInit.

realWorldUseCases: Putting side effects in the constructor makes component instantiation do "real work" before Angular finishes binding and before tests can set up spies.
followUpConfusion: If you must react to input changes over time, use ngOnChanges (or an input setter). DOM/ViewChild work usually belongs in ngAfterViewInit .

realWorldUseCases: GreatFrontEnd covers the lifecycle basics, but not the concrete side-effect placement or testability argument.
actionableExamples: Its interview examples are short and readable enough that the example comparison remains close.
followUpConfusion: The page does not spend much time on what to say next when inputs continue changing or when DOM access is required.

actionableExamples: Add one minimal fixture.detectChanges test example so the testability point becomes more concrete.

competitors.label: Stack Overflow: Difference between Constructor and ngOnInit
competitors.selectionReason: This is one of the most visited community Q&A pages for the exact constructor-vs-ngOnInit interview prompt.
competitors.winReason: The Stack Overflow thread is strong, but our page now combines a direct before-and-after code pair, explicit test timing, and the ngOnChanges follow-up in one cohesive answer.
theirStrengths: The accepted answer and comments cover several practical distinctions in one place, including @Input timing and the constructor-versus-ngOnInit split.
theirGaps: It is community-shaped rather than a single cohesive frontend-specific explanation.

followUpConfusion: If you must react to input changes over time, use ngOnChanges (or an input setter). DOM/ViewChild work usually belongs in ngAfterViewInit.

realWorldUseCases: The thread already frames constructor as DI/setup and ngOnInit as the place for real initialization work, so the practical gap is small.
actionableExamples: Multiple answers and comments provide concrete examples about @Input availability, ViewChild timing, and initialization placement, but our page now has a single before-and-after code path for constructor, ngOnInit, and ngOnChanges.
followUpConfusion: The thread also covers common follow-ups like ngOnChanges ordering and why constructor access to inputs is wrong, but our page now keeps those follow-ups in one tighter lifecycle decision model.

realWorldUseCases: Add one example where constructor work causes a premature API call before the test harness has configured spies.
